title: Quantum Geometric Quadrupole of Cooper Pairs
authors: Wenqin Chen, Kaijie Yang, Ting Cao, Shi-Zeng Lin, Jiabin Yu, Di Xiao
abstract: The size of Cooper pairs defines a fundamental length scale of superconductivity, conventionally set by band dispersion and the superconducting gap. This picture breaks down in flat bands, where quenched dispersion makes quantum geometry essential. Here we develop a general framework based on the Cooper pair quadrupole moment, whose trace gives the pair size. The framework holds for both dispersive and flat-band cases, and provides a unified description of the geometric origin of this length scale. In particular, when time-reversal symmetry is broken, Berry curvature enters through the phase structure of the pair wavefunction and gives an essential contribution absent from previous quantum-metric theories. Together, Berry curvature and quantum metric impose a geometric lower bound on the pair size. Applying this framework to rhombohedral graphene, we find that the Berry-curvature-induced contribution can dominate and yields pair sizes comparable to experimentally inferred coherence lengths. These results identify Berry curvature as a central geometric ingredient controlling the microscopic length scale of superconductivity.
summary: This paper develops a new framework to calculate the size of Cooper pairs in flat-band superconductors by analyzing the quantum geometric quadrupole moment. It reveals that Berry curvature and the quantum metric impose a fundamental lower bound on the pair size, which can dominate the coherence length in systems like rhombohedral graphene. This identifies quantum geometry as a central driver of the microscopic length scales in correlated electronic systems.
main_problem: Determining the fundamental length scale (size) of Cooper pairs in flat-band superconductors where conventional BCS theory fails due to quenched band dispersion.
main_result: The authors establish that the Cooper pair size is determined by a geometric lower bound arising from the quantum metric and Berry curvature, with the latter providing a significant contribution when time-revanl symmetry is broken.
method: A momentum-space derivation using a band-projected BCS framework, decomposing the pair quadrupole moment into amplitude, quantum metric, and phase structure components.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to (nearly) flat-band systems, specifically focusing on rhombohedral graphene and twisted moiré systems using a two-band effective Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Cooper pair size (trace of the quadrupole moment), pair Berry curvature, pair quantum metric, and pair geometric dipole.
important_parameters: Berry curvature, quantum metric, winding number, momentum cutoff, and the presence/absence of time-reversal symmetry.
main_assumptions: The theory assumes a band-projected BCS framework for intraband pairing, neglects band dispersion in the limit of local attractive interaction, and utilizes a tight-binding approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a semiclassical illustration of the two-body bound state and the roles of anomalous velocity and intrinsic spread; Figure 2 shows the two-band model for rhombohedral graphene and its low-energy dispersion.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of pair size in flat bands, develops a formal mathematical framework for the pair quadrupole moment, decomposes the pair size into three gauge-invariant components, establishes a geometric lower bound, and validates the theory using rhombohedral graphene and the 'Berry trashcan' model.
why_interesting: It provides a deep connection between topological invariants (Berry curvature/metric) and the microscopic spatial structure of many-body bound states, which is relevant to understanding the stability and scale of many-body correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Semiclassical picture of a two-body bound state in Bloch bands. Under an attractive interaction V , two electrons orbit each other with velocity vF + vA, where the anomalous velocity vA = ∇V × Ωarises from Berry curvature Ω. The separation d between two electron centers sets the size of the circular trajectory, while each electron’s intrinsic spread is characterized by the quantum metric gµν. Together, these two contributions determine the Cooper pair size.

FIG. 2. Cooper pair size in the two-band model of rhombohedral graphene. (a) Schematic of the two-band model formed by the two surface orbitals under a displacement field. (b) Low-energy band dispersion for the potential difference ∆= 80 meV, which is tunable by the displacement field. (c) Fermi level and Fermi momentum as functions of the electron density. (d) Phase vortex structure (color) of the envelope function and the pair geometric dipole dG (arrows). (e) Cooper pair size and its contributions as functions of the winding number N, which is varied as a model parameter. Here kC = 0.3 nm−1.
